schools.summary: Paul reminds them how he came: not with superior eloquence (excellency of speech) or philosophical wisdom — deliberately renouncing the rhetorical display the Greeks admired — declaring plainly the testimony of God (the witness God gave concerning Christ), content with the bare message, not adorned with human art (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Tertullian): Paul's plain manner was deliberate, that the gospel's power, not the preacher's skill, should be seen.

themes: came not with excellency of speech or of wisdom
declaring unto you the testimony of God
Paul's plain preaching
renunciation of rhetorical display

schools.summary: Paul made a deliberate determination (resolution) to confine his preaching to one theme — Jesus Christ — setting aside all else as his subject among them; the emphasis falling on the crucified Christ, the very point of offence to Jew and Greek (1:23), made the centre of all his preaching (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the whole of saving knowledge centres in the crucified Christ; Paul resolved to know and preach nothing else, the cross being the sum of the gospel.

themes: I determined not to know any thing among you, save Jesus Christ
and him crucified
Paul's resolve to know nothing but Christ crucified
the cross the sum of the gospel

schools.summary: Paul confesses he was with them in weakness (bodily frailty, or the absence of imposing presence), and in fear and much trembling — a deep sense of his own insufficiency and the weight of his task; not cowardice but a holy anxiety, a humble concern lest he fail, and dependence on God rather than self (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul's evident weakness made the power of his preaching manifestly God's, not his own — the treasure shining the brighter in an earthen vessel.

themes: I was with you in weakness, and in fear, and in much trembling
Paul's weakness, fear, and trembling
humble dependence on God
power seen as God's

schools.summary: Paul's speech and preaching were not with 'enticing (persuasive, alluring) words of man's wisdom' — not the artful rhetoric and plausible arguments of the philosophers — but 'in demonstration of the Spirit and of power,' the convincing, converting work of the Holy Spirit and the power (of miracles or the Spirit's inward energy) that attended the word (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): human eloquence persuades by art; the Spirit demonstrates by power, convincing the heart with a certainty no rhetoric can give.

themes: not with enticing words of man's wisdom
but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power
preaching in the Spirit's power
power over human eloquence

schools.summary: Paul's deliberate purpose in preaching plainly: that their faith should not rest upon (be founded on) human wisdom or eloquence — for a faith built on rhetoric would be unstable — but on the power of God, the divine power they had experienced in the Spirit's work; a faith so founded is sure and abiding (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: had faith rested on eloquence, it would stand or fall with the preacher's skill; resting on God's power, it is immovable.

themes: that your faith should not stand in the wisdom of men
but in the power of God
faith resting on God's power
the firm ground of saving faith

schools.summary: Paul, having disparaged human wisdom, affirms the gospel is true wisdom — spoken among 'them that are perfect' (the mature, established believers able to receive it), not the spiritually immature; not the wisdom of this world or its rulers (the great, the philosophers), a wisdom that 'comes to nought,' passing and perishing (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Calvin): the gospel is the highest wisdom, but only the spiritually mature discern it; the world's wisdom, however esteemed, is transient and empty.

themes: we speak wisdom among them that are perfect
not the wisdom of this world, nor of the princes of this world, that come to nought
the true wisdom among the mature
the gospel as the highest wisdom

schools.summary: Paul speaks 'the wisdom of God in a mystery' — a wisdom that is God's own, once hidden (a mystery, secret) but now revealed in the gospel; hidden in former ages and ordained (fore-appointed, decreed) by God before the world began, an eternal plan, not a later device (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): God ordained this hidden wisdom 'unto our glory' — the salvation and glorification of believers being its appointed purpose.

themes: we speak the wisdom of God in a mystery
the hidden wisdom, which God ordained before the world unto our glory
the hidden wisdom of God
God's eternal plan for our glory

schools.summary: The rulers' ignorance is proved by the crucifixion: had they understood God's hidden wisdom and Christ's true dignity, they would not have crucified him (Acts 3:17). 'The Lord of glory' — a title applied to Jehovah in the Old Testament (Psalm 24) — is here given to the crucified Christ, a striking contrast between the indignity of the cross and the majesty of the Victim (Barnes, Robertson).

Gill, Calvin: the 'princes of this world' are the human rulers who crucified Christ — the Jewish leaders (Scribes, Pharisees, priests) and the Roman authorities (Pilate); Gill explicitly rejects the view that demonic powers are meant, taking it of the ecclesiastical and civil rulers blinded by ignorance.

Wesley: the giving of this august title 'the Lord of glory,' peculiar to the great Jehovah, plainly shows Christ to be the supreme God — as the Father is the Father of glory and the Spirit the Spirit of glory, the title applied to all three showing Father, Son, and Holy Ghost to be the one God of glory.

debates.question: Who are the 'princes of this world' who crucified the Lord of glory?

sides.position: The human rulers — the Jewish leaders and Roman authorities (Pilate, Herod)
Some have understood it of the demonic powers, a reading Gill explicitly rejects

themes: none of the princes of this world knew
they would not have crucified the Lord of glory
the rulers' ignorance
the deity of the Lord of glory

schools.summary: Paul quotes (freely, the sense of) Isaiah 64:4 (with 65:17; 52:15): things unseen, unheard, unimagined by man. The primary reference is NOT future heaven but the present gospel blessings revealed now by the Spirit — the wisdom and grace of the gospel; for v.10 ('but God hath revealed them unto us') proves they are already disclosed, not merely awaited (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson).

Calvin, Gill: the words refer to the doctrines of grace and the mysteries of the gospel now revealed, not (primarily) the future heavenly state; Calvin understands them of the gifts of God's grace daily conferred on believers, viewed in their source — God's fatherly love — and thus leading on to eternal life.

Wesley: the words, fulfilled in the blessings of the Messiah's kingdom, describe what no natural man has seen, heard, or known — the things God hath prepared for them that love him; Clarke insists they belong to the present state, the wondrous light and liberty the gospel communicates.

debates.question: Do 'eye hath not seen' the things prepared refer to future heavenly glory or the present gospel revelation?

sides.position: Primarily the present gospel blessings/wisdom revealed now by the Spirit (v.10 'God hath revealed them unto us')
The popular application to future heaven, allowed by extension as a secondary sense

themes: Eye hath not seen, nor ear heard
the things which God hath prepared for them that love him
things beyond natural discovery
present gospel revelation or future glory

schools.summary: What natural reason cannot discover (v.9), God has revealed to us — first to the apostles, then to all believers — by his Spirit; and the Spirit searches (knows thoroughly, fathoms) all things, even the deep things (profound counsels and nature) of God, a knowledge so complete it proves the Spirit's omniscience and deity (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Henry): that the Spirit searches the deep things of God demonstrates his Godhead and distinct personhood — the Holy Spirit one, indivisible, who knows the mysteries; none but God can know God thus.

themes: God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit
the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the deep things of God
the Spirit revealing and searching
the deity of the Holy Spirit

schools.summary: The comparison: a man's own inmost thoughts are known only to his own spirit (his self-consciousness), no other fully knowing them; just so, the deep things of God are known only to the Spirit of God — the Spirit, being God, alone has perfect knowledge of the divine mind (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril: the analogy implies the Spirit's deity and his intimate relation to God, as a man's spirit to himself; only one who is God can know God's depths.

themes: what man knoweth the things of a man, save the spirit of man which is in him
the things of God knoweth no man, but the Spirit of God
only the Spirit knows the things of God
the Spirit's divine knowledge

schools.summary: Believers have received not the spirit of the world (the temper and wisdom of the unregenerate world) but the Spirit which is of God — the Holy Spirit himself, given to them — that they might know (understand, appreciate) the things freely (graciously) bestowed on them by God, the blessings of salvation discerned by the Spirit's light (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the indwelling Spirit enables believers to recognize and enjoy the grace given them; what the world cannot know, the Spirit makes known to God's people.

themes: not the spirit of the world, but the spirit which is of God
that we might know the things that are freely given to us of God
receiving the Spirit to know God's gifts
the Spirit making known grace

schools.summary: The apostles speak these revealed things not in words taught by human wisdom (rhetoric, philosophy) but in words the Holy Spirit teaches — the very language, as well as the matter, being Spirit-given; 'comparing spiritual things with spiritual' meaning to expound spiritual truths in spiritual words, or interpret spiritual things to spiritual persons (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): divine truth is best, and only rightly, expressed in the words the Spirit supplies, and discerned by the spiritually minded.

themes: not in the words which man's wisdom teacheth, but which the Holy Ghost teacheth
comparing spiritual things with spiritual
the Spirit-taught words
spiritual matter in spiritual words

schools.summary: The 'natural man' (psychikos) is the unregenerate man, governed by the soul's natural faculties and senses without the Spirit (opposed to the pneumatikos, spiritual man); he does not receive (rejects, has no relish for) the things of God's Spirit, counting them foolishness, because they are 'spiritually discerned.' Barnes qualifies: he cannot perceive their beauty while he remains sensual and in love with sin, but this does not prove he lacks the natural faculty — his inability is his strong love of sin (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin, Gill: total inability — the natural man not only will not but cannot know spiritual things; it is owing not merely to obstinacy of will but to the impotency of the understanding that man does not attain the things of the Spirit; faith is not in one's own power but divinely conferred, all mankind being by nature destitute of the Spirit of God.

Wesley: the natural man is every man who has not the Spirit, with no way of knowing but his senses and natural understanding; the things of the Spirit are foolishness to him — as he has not the will, so neither has he the power, because they are discerned only by spiritual senses which he has not.

debates.question: Does the natural man's not knowing spiritual things prove a total inability or only moral unwillingness?

sides.position: Total inability — he cannot know them, faith being divinely conferred, all by nature destitute of the Spirit
He cannot perceive their beauty while in love with sin, yet retains the natural faculty; the inability is his love of sin
As he lacks the will, so he lacks the power and the spiritual senses

themes: the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God
neither can he know them, because they are spiritually discerned
the natural man's inability
the inability crux

schools.summary: The 'spiritual' man (the regenerate, indwelt and taught by the Spirit) 'judgeth' (discerns, examines, rightly estimates) all things — he perceives and weighs spiritual realities the natural man cannot; yet he himself is 'judged of no man,' that is, of no natural man, the unregenerate lacking the discernment to estimate or sit in judgment on him (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the believer, enlightened by the Spirit, has a discernment the world lacks and cannot itself be truly assessed by those without the Spirit — not above all examination, but beyond the competence of the merely natural.

themes: he that is spiritual judgeth all things
yet he himself is judged of no man
the spiritual man's discernment
Spirit-given insight

schools.summary: Paul quotes Isaiah 40:13 — no one has known the mind of the Lord so as to instruct or counsel him, God's mind being beyond all human teaching; yet 'we' (believers, taught by the Spirit) 'have the mind of Christ,' sharing through the Spirit in Christ's own mind and understanding of divine things (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the believer, by the Spirit, is admitted to know what is hidden from the world — possessing the mind of Christ, he understands the things of God.

themes: who hath known the mind of the Lord, that he may instruct him
but we have the mind of Christ
the believer having the mind of Christ
Spirit-given participation in Christ's mind
